chunk_summary: This section differentiates perceived low milk supply from true low production, emphasizing clinical assessment of infant feeding behaviors, maternal risk factors, and management strategies to protect breastfeeding and milk production.
learning_objectives: Identify normal newborn behaviors that may be misinterpreted as hunger or low milk supply.
Assess infant feeding effectiveness using objective indicators such as latch, swallowing, diaper counts, and weight trajectory.
Recognize maternal risk factors for low milk production and their clinical implications.
Develop management plans to address true low milk supply while avoiding the supplement-supply spiral.
key_facts: Perceived low milk supply is more common than true low production.
Objective indicators include diaper counts, weight trajectory, and supervised test-weighing.
Maternal risk factors include delayed lactogenesis, retained placental tissue, untreated hypothyroidism, diabetes, and prior breast surgery.
Signs of insufficient glandular tissue include widely spaced breasts with minimal growth during pregnancy.
Management should include latch correction, scheduled milk removal, and time-bound reassessment to avoid open-ended formula use.
reference_quote: Perceived low milk supply is far more common than true insufficient production. Normal newborn behaviors such as frequent feeding, evening cluster feeds, and wanting to be held are often misread as hunger.
document_key_topics: Perceived vs true low milk supply
Maternal risk factors for low milk production
